metadata.summary: 2026 update: continuous validation, not one-time gates. Rank evidence on 5-level hierarchy. Validate with tier-1/2 from non-network. Re-validate quarterly.

content.title: Problem Validation 2026
content.summary: An updated problem validation methodology for 2026 emphasizing continuous evidence-based validation over one-time gates. Core rule: rank all evidence on a 5-level hierarchy (paid > committed > engaged with prototype > expressed interest > stated problem); only declare a problem validated when ≥3 tier-1 or tier-2 signals come from cold, non-network respondents. Validation is an ongoing process, not a checklist to pass once.

why.p: Survey-only validation and warm-contact interviews systematically over-report demand. Behavioral commitments (payment, sign-up, prototype time) are the only reliable signals. LLM-generated "synthetic user" data produces plausible but worthless results. The 2026 update reflects that AI-adjacent markets shift rapidly; re-validation is required every quarter to catch invalidated assumptions before they drive quarter-long sprints.

list.item: Pre-MVP: validate problem is real, painful, and worth paying for before writing code.
Pivoting: re-validate problem assumptions when retention is low or engagement is sparse.
Adjacent expansion: testing whether an existing segment has a related underserved problem.
After a hypothesis breaks (low conversion, no upsell) to confirm the problem still holds.
PMF is established with a paying user base — switch to feature-discovery and continuous-discovery.
Incremental funnel optimization where A/B testing answers faster.
Cannot reach the target segment within a week — you will over-rely on weak proxies.
Commodity or undifferentiated problems where solution quality matters more than problem existence.

section.name: evidence-hierarchy
section.p: The 5-level validation evidence hierarchy with explicit weighting rules, commitment signal types, and how to avoid common signal mis-classification errors.

rule.statement: Declare a problem validated only when at least 3 tier-1 or tier-2 signals come from cold, non-network respondents. Any conclusion drawn from fewer or weaker signals is a hypothesis, not a validation.
rule.rationale: Behavioral commitments (payment, sign-up) are costly signals that predict actual adoption. Stated preferences are cheap and optimistic. The 2026 update adds the non-network requirement because warm contacts systematically over-report demand.

list.item: Level 1 (strongest): users paid for a solution — actual purchase behavior.
Level 2: users committed — signed up, provided a letter of intent, invested significant time.
Level 3: users engaged deeply with a prototype — used it, provided feedback, returned.
Level 4: users expressed strong interest — stated intent, agreed to follow-up.
Level 5 (weakest): users said they have the problem — acknowledgment without any action.

rule.statement: Weight tier-1 and tier-2 signals from non-network respondents 5x higher than equivalent signals from warm contacts.
rule.rationale: Network contacts compliment to preserve the relationship. Cold strangers who pay or commit are providing unbiased, costly signals.

list.item: Time commitment: agreed to a follow-up, spent 30+ minutes in a prototype, introduced you to another potential user.
Reputation commitment: introduced you to their boss or team lead; named you in a group message.
Money commitment: paid a pre-order deposit; signed a letter of intent with decision-maker approval.

antipattern.description: Treating "I would definitely introduce you to my team" as a level-2 commitment signal.
antipattern.reason: Future-tense statements are level-4 interest at best. Level-2 commitment requires the introduction to have actually happened (past tense + evidence).

rule.statement: Re-validate problem assumptions every quarter; AI-adjacent markets shift rapidly and validated problems from 6 months ago may no longer hold.
rule.rationale: Validation is not a one-time gate. Market conditions, competitor solutions, and regulation change the problem landscape. A quarterly check prevents building on outdated assumptions.

section.name: interview-protocol
section.p: Mom Test question rules for 2025-2026, the Vision-Framing-Weakness-Pedestal-Ask opener, the 3-agent agentic workflow, and known LLM failure modes in validation work.

rule.statement: Ask about the interviewee's past life and current behavior; never ask about your idea or their hypothetical future.
rule.rationale: Questions about the idea trigger politeness bias and produce compliments, not data. Past behavior is the only reliable predictor of future behavior.

list.item: "How do you currently solve X?" — not "Would you use this?"
"Tell me about the last time you dealt with X." — not "Is this a problem for you?"
"What have you tried? What did it cost?" — not "Would you pay $X?"
"When was the last time you faced this problem?" — not "Do you think this is a good idea?"

rule.statement: End every interview with a behavioral ask (intro, follow-up, prototype access); the yes/no response is the final data point of the session.
rule.rationale: A behavioral ask at the end converts the interview itself into a level-2 commitment signal. A "yes" that requires effort (an actual introduction, not a vague "sure") is meaningful data.

list.item: Vision: state the problem you want to solve, in 1-2 sentences.
Framing: "I have nothing to sell — I'm just trying to understand the problem."
Weakness: "I might be completely wrong about this, and that's actually what I'm hoping to find out."
Pedestal: "You're exactly the person whose experience matters most here."
Ask: "Would you be willing to spend 20 minutes telling me about your experience with X?"

antipattern.description: Agents skip the Weakness step when generating the opener, producing a confident pitch instead of a vulnerability statement.
antipattern.reason: The Weakness step creates psychological safety for the respondent to give critical feedback. Without it, the opener reads as a pitch and respondents perform agreement.

section.p: The agentic workflow uses three serial stages: (1) validation-interviewer (sonnet): drafts segment-specific Mom Test scripts and follow-up probes from the problem hypothesis. (2) signal-extractor (sonnet): scans each transcript, tags commitments (time/reputation/money) and red flags (compliment/hypothetical/generic), outputs structured JSON. (3) validation-synthesizer (opus or sonnet): aggregates 10+ signal_extraction.json files, produces evidence ledger sorted by tier 1 to 5, refuses to label problem "validated" unless 3+ distinct tier-1/2 evidences from non-network respondents.

rule.statement: Never let the synthesizer draft the validation conclusion and the evidence ledger in one pass; split into two separate calls with human review of the ledger between them.
rule.rationale: Agents that produce ledger and conclusion in one pass anchor the conclusion on the first evidence they process. Human review of the ledger before the conclusion prevents this ordering bias.

list.item: Agent treats enthusiastic compliments as validation and upgrades them to level-3 commitment signals. Compliments are red flags, not signals. Encode "compliments are red flags" directly in the system prompt; do not rely on the model's implicit knowledge.

Whisper/auto-transcription mis-attributes speaker turns, mixing interviewer questions into respondent quotes. Signal extraction from mis-attributed turns produces false commitment signals. Require diarized transcription (speaker labels) before running the signal-extractor agent.

Mom Test rewriting agent subtly converts behavioral past-tense questions into hypothetical future-tense questions. This is a known LLM pattern — the model defaults to polite hypothetical forms from training data. Validate every agent-generated script against a "no would/could/might" checklist before use.

section.name: execution-and-best-practices
section.p: How to run validation as a structured workflow and critical best practices.

Run validation as three serial loops: (1) recruit + interview using Mom Test scripts, (2) extract commitment signals + red flags from transcripts, (3) synthesize an evidence ledger that ranks by signal strength. Use a structured-output JSON schema with fields {segment, problem_statement, evidence_tier, citation, signal_type}. Human-in-loop is mandatory before declaring problem validated — the agent's job is evidence aggregation, not the conclusion.

list.item: Keep an evidence ledger with tier-1 to tier-5 signals; the only "validated" label requires tier-1/2 from non-network respondents.
Recruit cold (Reddit/LinkedIn DMs) for at least half of interviews; otherwise survivorship bias dominates.
Open every interview with "I have nothing to sell"; agents tend to drop this and the conversation pivots to product pitch.
End every interview with a behavioral ask (intro, follow-up, prototype access). The yes/no is the data.
Re-validate every quarter — markets shift, regulation shifts, AI shifts adjacent products.
Agents anthropomorphize compliments as validation. Encode the rule "compliments are red flags" into the prompt.
Whisper transcripts mis-attribute speakers; require diarization before signal extraction or you'll mix interviewer prompts into respondent quotes.
LLMs will summarize an interview with confident generalizations from a single quote — require frequency counts before any "users want X" claim.
Don't let the agent draft the validation report and decision in one pass; split: (1) ledger, (2) human-reviewed conclusion.
Mom Test rewriting is a known LLM weakness — the agent will subtly turn behavioral questions into hypothetical ones. Validate the script with a checklist before sending.
Stated preferences ("I would pay") are unreliable signals; only behavioral commitments count.
Friends and warm contacts give compliments; weight non-network respondents 5x.
Survey-only validation systematically over-reports demand. Pair with behavioral signals (LOI, pre-order, time spent in prototype).
"Vision-Framing-Weakness-Pedestal-Ask" framing helps but is easy to mis-execute; agents tend to skip the weakness step.
5-10 interviews give themes, not statistical evidence; 30+ for any quantitative claim.

template.purpose: Python script: scans interview transcript for compliment/hypothetical/generic red-flag language patterns.
Prompt for a signal-extractor subagent: extracts commitments and red flags from transcripts as structured JSON.
